adjustments: Gudmundsson used as one of the three centre-backs
Elanga and Isak appearing as wide forwards
phases.summary: Sweden began in a 3-4-2-1 and made the early running, winning a couple of corners and showing more of the ball. Japan were content to absorb pressure and break on the counter, creating a more open pattern overall, with Maeda’s direct run at Gudmundsson and an early Bernhardsson effort at Suzuki standing out as the main individual moments.

formations_blocks: Japan probing and building pressure after a slow start
Sweden in a more cautious, indecisive setup without a clearly committed pressing/block structure
adjustments: Hydration break paused a flat, stop-start spell in the game
Sweden forced into a defensive change: Lucas Bergvall on for Isak Hien, with Victor Lindelöf dropping back into defence
Japan forced into a change of their own: Shogo Taniguchi on for Ko Itakura
key_matchups: Doan vs Stroud/Gudmundsson on the blocked long-range shot
Nakamura and overlapping Ito combining on Japan's left against Sweden's defending line
Ueda vs Hien, including the challenge that led to Hien’s yellow-card foul
Hien vs Japan’s right-sided cross before his injury
phases.summary: Japan grew into the match after a slow opening, with more sustained pressure, a left-sided overload through Nakamura and Ito, and a first shot on target from Sugawara. Sweden, by contrast, looked uncertain and then lost Hien to injury, forcing Lindelöf deeper and a reshuffle that added to the game’s confused, stop-start feel; Japan also had to replace the injured Itakura before half-time.

The phase is simply the half-time break in a goalless match, with no tactical information provided beyond the restart of the second half. Japan begin the second half unchanged, indicating no personnel or structural adjustment at the interval.

adjustments: Japan's right-sided combination play through Sugawara, Doan and Ueda created the move for Maeda's goal
Sweden responded more directly, with Elanga cutting inside from the right to shoot from the edge of the area
Japan continued to find central and half-space entries around the box, while Sweden looked for set-piece or isolated attacking moments
phases.summary: Japan controlled the early part of the phase through slick right-side link play and a well-timed run from Maeda to take the lead. Sweden then equalised almost immediately through Elanga’s individual quality from the right, shifting the feel of the game from Japan’s combination attacks to a more open, end-to-end contest.

formations_blocks: Sweden pushing on in a strong attacking spell
Japan defending the late phase under pressure
adjustments: Sweden made a double substitution: Ken Sema and Daniel Svensson on for Bernhardsson and Stroud
Japan made a double substitution: Yuto Nagatomo and Tsuyoshi Watanabe on for Ayumu Seko and Keito Nakamura
Sweden later introduced Carl Starfelt for Victor Lindelof due to cramp and Benjamin Nygren for Gabriel Gudmundsson
Japan's changes appeared to refresh the back line and wide positions late on
key_matchups: Kamada vs Sweden's late defensive line
phases.summary: Sweden finished strongly and spent much of the closing spell on the front foot, creating a series of set-piece and open-play chances while Japan were forced deeper. Both sides made changes to manage fatigue and protect the result, but Sweden came closest to winning in stoppage time, with Elanga and then Isak denied by superb saves from Suzuki, including Isak's header onto the bar.
